finding.title: doom_knight easy-verdict CONFIRMED: 97.4% win over 38 decks, but it is the 2nd-HARDEST boss in the rotation (curve is inverted, not doom_knight being too hard)
finding.evidence: boss-report.json doomKnightCheck: nDecks 38, fracWin 0.974, meanCushionOnWin 0.592, single loser = mage-pure-burn-boss1-2 (8 deaths). enemy-summary doom_knight: scaledHP 380, scaledDamage 6, atkCD 2500 (rawDPS 2.40), affinity counter, enrage@0.5 x1.4. Across the rotation the SAME archetypes get EASIER, not harder, after boss1: armor_finisher 0.65->1.00 (b1->b2), pure_poison 0.84->0.85->0.85->0.94, slow_stun_control 0.53->1.00, mixed-bruiser 0.54->0.65->0.74. doom_knight's 0.592 mean cushion is the lowest of all bosses except infernal_dragon (0.601). Its difficulty comes from 380 HP being slow to chew through at L6 decks + counter-affinity retaliation, NOT from its low damage (2.4 DPS).
finding.diagnosis: doom_knight is correctly EASY in absolute terms (>97% win, the one loser is the known-weak pure_burn mage opening cold against 380 HP with counter retaliation). The realism check PASSES. The real problem is relative: doom_knight sits at the bottom of the cushion ranking, so the player's FIRST boss is among the most HP-threatening fights they will ever face, and bosses 2-5 are softer than the tutorial boss. The fix is not to touch doom_knight (it must stay easy per the brief) but to make the later bosses ramp ABOVE it.
finding.proposed_change: Leave doom_knight unchanged (it is the calibrated baseline and passes the easy-check). Treat its 0.59 cushion as the floor that the rest of the rotation must exceed downward (i.e. later bosses should land progressively BELOW 0.59). Bosses can be tuned freely under the charter, so no C1-C5 card lever is touched.

verdict.title: doom_knight easy-check PASSES and the inverted-boss-curve diagnosis is REAL, but the title misreads its own data: by mean cushion doom_knight is the HARDEST boss (0.592), not "2nd-hardest"; and the aggregate ranking is partly a deck-pool-composition artifact
verdict.reason: All quoted numbers reproduce exactly. boss-report.json doomKnightCheck: nDecks 38, fracWin 0.974, meanCushionOnWin 0.592, single loser = mage-pure-burn-boss1-2 (8 deaths). enemy-summary doom_knight: scaledHP 380, scaledDamage 6 (rawDPS 6/2.5=2.40), affinity counter, enrage@0.5 x1.4. The per-archetype trajectories (deck-quality-controlled) all check out and ALL rise after boss1: armor_finisher 0.653->1.0, slow_stun_control 0.532->1.0, mixed-bruiser 0.536->0.649->0.738, pure_poison 0.843->0.855->0.855->0.945. The substantive diagnosis -- 'the first boss is more HP-threatening per equivalent deck than the later bosses; the curve is inverted' -- is therefore SUPPORTED and the easy-check verdict (>97% win, lone loser is the known-weak pure_burn mage) is correct. doom_knight should NOT be touched. TWO corrections to the finding's characterization: (1) The title calls doom_knight 'the 2nd-HARDEST boss', but the aggregate boss-cushion ranking is doom_knight 0.592 < infernal_dragon 0.601 < boss_iron_golem 0.805 < iron_golem 0.818 < lizard_king 0.822 < bog_witch 0.888 < desert_golem 0.895 -- so by the very metric it cites, doom_knight is the LOWEST/HARDEST cushion, not 2nd. The evidence text ('lowest of all bosses except infernal_dragon') contradicts the title; 0.592<0.601 makes it the lowest. (2) The 0.592 aggregate is partly a CONFOUND: doom_knight (n=38) is the only boss faced by the full weak/starter pool (pure_burn cushion 0.008, poison_burn 0.134, poison 0.173, burn 0.37, heal 0.504, *-weak starters), which drags its mean down, while deep bosses (n=7-9) are faced ONLY by tuned endgame decks. The per-archetype trajectory is the correct (deck-controlled) evidence and it stands; the aggregate cushion comparison overstates the gap. The proposed_change is C1-C5-safe (touches no card; bosses are tuneable under the charter), but its literal target 'later bosses should land progressively BELOW 0.59' is too blunt -- it should be archetype-matched, not aggregate-matched, since deep bosses face only strong decks. As a boss_progression finding, no swap-test sim is required; data_recheck is the appropriate method and it confirms the substance with the noted relabeling.

finding.title: Deep-boss cushions rest on thin, self-selected deck samples (7-9 decks, all relatively tuned T3 builds) — directionally solid but quantitatively soft
finding.evidence: nDecks: desert_golem 9, infernal_dragon 9, boss_iron_golem 7 (vs doom_knight 38, iron_golem 23). The deep matchups are dominated by strong 'deep-endgame' and 'late-strong' archetypes (pure-poison, slow-stun-control, armor-finisher) that are near-optimal, so their high cushions partly reflect strong piloting, not just soft bosses. boss-prog-final.json shows the same decks CAN be pressured: late-mage-burn-strong__vs__lizard_king = 5 DEATHS / winRate 0 (the curve has real teeth for off-meta builds), and late-mage-burn-strong__vs__bog_witch cushion 0.138.
finding.diagnosis: The under-threatening conclusion for b2-b5 is robust (it holds even for the strongest archetypes and is corroborated by the inert-behavior code analysis and the fixed-archetype cross-stage trend). But the EXACT cushion magnitudes at b5-b7 should be treated as upper bounds with wide error bars, and the proposed boss buffs must be re-validated against a BROADER deck set (including mid-strength and off-meta builds) so the ramp does not accidentally make a deep boss unwinnable for a realistic average deck.
finding.proposed_change: Before committing the boss-buff numbers, expand the deep-stage deck battery to >=15-20 decks per boss spanning weak/avg/strong builds and both classes (mirror the boss1 sample breadth), re-sim with >=6 repeats, and tune bossMult/damage/behavior so the AVERAGE deck lands ~0.45-0.55 cushion with winRate >=0.8 and the weakest realistic decks stay above ~0.6 winRate. This is a validation gate, not a card change.

verdict.title: Deep-boss cushions rest on thin, self-selected strong-deck samples (7-9 decks, all tuned late/deep-endgame builds) — directionally solid, quantitatively soft
verdict.reason: VERIFIED and correctly characterized. Every cited number is accurate against the source JSONs. boss-report.json confirms nDecks: desert_golem 9, infernal_dragon 9, boss_iron_golem 7 vs doom_knight 38, iron_golem 23 — i.e. deep bosses have 18-25% of the first boss's deck breadth. The compositional claim is the crux and it holds: archetype-summary.json shows the boss5/6/7 samples contain ONLY tuned late/deep-endgame builds (armor_finisher, slow_stun_control, poison+block, pure-poison, detonator-control, mixed-elemental-toolbox, mixed-bruiser) with NO weak/starter archetypes, whereas the boss1 (n=38) sample spans starter-physical-weak, armor-heavy-weak, pure_burn, heal, poison etc. So the high deep-boss cushions (desert_golem 0.895, boss_iron_golem 0.805) partly reflect near-optimal piloting, not just soft bosses — a real selection effect. The corroborating evidence is also quoted correctly: boss-prog-final.json line 906-932 has late-mage-burn-strong__vs__lizard_king = 5 deaths / winRate 0, and line 934-960 has late-mage-burn-strong__vs__bog_witch cushion 0.138, demonstrating the deep curve still has teeth for off-meta builds. The diagnosis follows logically: the DIRECTIONAL under-threatening conclusion for b2-b5 is robust (it holds even for the strongest archetypes — every strong deck wins with large cushion — and is corroborated by the fixed-archetype cross-stage trend and inert-behavior code analysis), but the EXACT cushion magnitudes are upper bounds with wide error bars. Two honest caveats that the finding slightly understates but that only reinforce it: (1) even within the strong samples there is genuine downside spread (infernal_dragon detonator-control cushion 0.259 and mixed-bruiser 0.645; boss_iron_golem detonator-control 0.664), and (2) deep bosses are NOT mechanically inert — scaledHP climbs steeply (infernal_dragon 575, boss_iron_golem 611) and they carry live behaviors (enrage, multi_hit, shield 10-12, drain), so the soft cushions are as plausibly driven by generous deep-stage hero runXP (boss7 runXP 4400/~L19) as by under-tuned bosses — exactly the ambiguity this caveat flags. The proposed change is a VALIDATION GATE not a mechanical claim, so it cannot violate C1-C5: it asks to broaden the deep-deck battery to >=15-20 decks/boss spanning weak/avg/strong and both classes, re-sim >=6 repeats, and only THEN tune bossMult/damage/behavior (enemy-progression tuning, which the brief explicitly authorizes) so an average deck lands ~0.45-0.55 cushion at winRate>=0.8 while the weakest realistic deck stays >0.6. This correctly prevents a boss buff calibrated on strong-only samples from making a deep boss unwinnable for a realistic average deck. low severity is appropriate: it is a confidence/methodology qualifier on other findings, not an independent balance defect.
verdict.sim_evidence: No new sim required (methodology/sample-quality caveat, not an off_curve/tier_violation card claim). Re-read confirms: boss-report.json nDecks {doom_knight:38, iron_golem:23, lizard_king:22, bog_witch:15, desert_golem:9, infernal_dragon:9, boss_iron_golem:7} and meanCushionOnWin {iron_golem:0.818, lizard_king:0.822, bog_witch:0.888, desert_golem:0.895, infernal_dragon:0.601, boss_iron_golem:0.805}. archetype-summary.json deep-boss composition (all tuned builds, no weak ones): desert_golem(boss5)=armor_finisher 1.0 / mixed-bruiser 0.775 / poison_burn 0.68 / slow_stun_control 1.0 / poison+block 1.0,0.995 / mixed-elemental 0.791,0.816; infernal_dragon(boss6)=mixed-bruiser 0.645 / slow_stun_control 1.0 / detonator-control 0.599,0.259 / mixed-elemental 0.561; boss_iron_golem(boss7)=detonator-control 0.664,0.924 / mixed-elemental 0.785 / deep-endgame pure-poison 0.843 / deep-endgame slow-stun-control 0.924. Teeth-on-off-meta corroboration from boss-prog-final.json: late-mage-burn-strong__vs__lizard_king wins 0/5 deaths 5 winRate 0 (scaledHP 388), and late-mage-burn-strong__vs__bog_witch winRate 1 but cushion 0.138 / avgTtkMs 53000. enemy-summary.json confirms deep bosses are not inert: desert_golem scaledHP 540 + shield 10; infernal_dragon scaledHP 575 + multi_hit×3 + enrage; boss_iron_golem scaledHP 611 + shield 12 + enrage.

finding.title: T3 Quench Lance is in-deck WORSE than the T2 burn filler it competes with
finding.evidence: Swap test (8 fixed strong-burn cards + 1 candidate, vs late normal battery): swap-quenchlance-late avgTTK 13975 ms / avgHP% 0.323, SLOWER and squishier than the T2 baseline swap-baseline-late (Flame Dart) avgTTK 11869 / avgHP% 0.348; worst vs mutated_salamander ttk 21500, hp% 0.101. card-appearance t3-fire-fire-water peakOPS 8.33 (below T3 median 12.3). Card: 'Apply 2[burn]([int]). For 6s, if you consume 10+ [burn], gain 2[int] this combat (max 4).' cost {stamina:1,mana:1}, cd 2.4.
finding.diagnosis: Charter requires T3 to out-ceiling ANY T2. Quench Lance applies only 2 burn (same as a T2) on a 2.4s CD, and its INT-ramp gate (consume 10+ burn) rarely fires inside a pure-burn shell, so its conditional ceiling never realizes. Net: a T3 that loses to the T2 it replaces.
finding.proposed_change: C1 / T3>T2: buff the T3, never nerf Flame Dart. Cut Quench Lance cd 2.4s -> 1.6s (it is a banker/ramp card; snappy CD per C1) and raise base application 2[burn] -> 3[burn] so it out-banks the T2. Lower the INT-gain threshold 10 -> 6 consumed so the synergy actually triggers in-shell. Keep cost 1s+1m.

verdict.title: T3 Quench Lance is in-deck worse than T2 Flame Dart — REAL, but the cause is a DEAD INT-gate (stack_consumed never fires), not a too-high threshold; the proposed thr10→6 cut does nothing and the buff only reaches parity
verdict.reason: HEADLINE CONFIRMED via swap test: in a fixed late burn-mage shell (runXP720/INT3, candidate in slot 1, identical 9-card deck), current Quench Lance is consistently SLOWER and no tankier than the T2 Flame Dart it competes with — vs mutated_salamander TTK 11400 vs 10200 ms and cushion 0.71 vs 0.768; vs fire_elemental 6300 vs 5100 ms (0.87 vs 0.87); vs depths_horror ~5833 vs 5000 ms. Win 1.0 for both (these normals aren't lethal), so TTK is the discriminator and Quench Lance loses on it across all 3 enemies. peakOPS 8.33 is the 23rd percentile of T3 and the floor among offensive (magic/attack) T3 burn cards (the only lower ones are C3 defense/armor builds). So a damage-archetype T3 being strictly dominated by a T2 burn card is a genuine off-curve-weak / T3<T2 situation. HOWEVER the finding's diagnosis and fix are partly WRONG: (1) it quotes 'T3 median 12.3' but tier-ops-perf.json median is 15.33 (card-appearance ~16.5). (2) Its diagnosis ('INT-ramp rarely fires') understates reality — the gate fires NEVER in-shell: a no-ramp diagnostic (effects[1] INT-gate stripped to a pure 2-burn) is byte-identical to current (mutated_salamander 11400/0.71/188dmg == 11400/0.71/188; fire_elemental 6300 == 6300), proving the conditional ceiling contributes exactly zero even with Quench Lance placed right before a Supernova detonator. (3) The proposed threshold cut 10→6 does NOT fix it: a thr6-only override is identical to current (11400 == 11400). Root cause is the stack_consumed-emission gap already CODE-CONFIRMED in static-confirmed.json #9 — Pyre-keyword detonators (Pyre/Cinderlance/Cinder Sprint) emit no stack_consumed event, so they cannot feed Quench Lance's event_counter{stack_consumed,burn,min_amount}; only consume_stack_value detonators (Supernova/Tremor Detonate) emit it, and even Supernova does not trip the 6s-window counter here. (4) Even simulating the ramp fully realized (hero forced to INT7 = base3+4) Quench Lance reaches 7500 ms vs mutated_salamander / 4900 ms vs fire_elemental but STILL loses to Flame Dart at INT7 (6300 / 3700 ms), because Quench Lance has ZERO direct damage while Flame Dart deals 5 direct + 2 burn. So the proposed cd1.6/3burn/thr6 buff only reaches PARITY (confirmed run-1 buffed variant: 10800/5500/5283 ms ≈ baseline 10400/5100/5267), NOT the charter-required 'T3 out-ceilings ANY T2'. Keeping severity MED (an unused-tier offensive T3, not oppressive/game-breaking), but the FIX must change: drop the dead threshold tweak; instead (a) fix the engine so stack_consumed fires from the Pyre auto-consume path so the ramp can actually trigger (the real wiring bug, per C4 wiring-first), AND (b) give Quench Lance a small direct-damage clause or a stronger guaranteed banker payoff plus the cd1.6 cut so it can out-ceiling the T2 once its synergy lands — never nerf Flame Dart (C1/T3>T2). The bare cd1.6+3burn buff alone is insufficient.
verdict.sim_evidence: Swap spec tests/audit/val/swap/quenchlance2.json -> results tests/audit/val/swap/quenchlance2-res.json (6 repeats, fixed slot-1 candidate, runXP720, INT3 unless noted). vs mutated_salamander: ql2-baseline-flamedart ttk10200/hp0.768/dmg187; ql2-quench-current ttk11400/hp0.71/dmg188; ql2-quench-noramp(gate stripped) ttk11400/hp0.71/dmg188 (IDENTICAL=gate dead); ql2-quench-thr6only ttk11400/hp0.71/dmg188 (IDENTICAL=thr cut useless); ql2-quench-int7ceiling ttk7500/hp0.826/dmg192; ql2-flamedart-int7 ttk6300/hp0.884/dmg187. vs fire_elemental: baseline 5100/0.87; quench-current 6300/0.87; noramp 6300; thr6only 6300; int7ceiling 4900; flamedart-int7 3700/0.935. vs depths_horror: baseline 5000/0.928; quench-current 5833/0.928; noramp 5567; thr6only 6167; int7ceiling 5350; flamedart-int7 3700/0.964. Earlier spec tests/audit/val/swap/quenchlance.json -> quenchlance-res.json shows proposed buff (cd1.6/3burn/thr6) only reaches PARITY with the T2: ql-quench-buffed mutated_salamander 10800 / fire_elemental 5500 / depths_horror 5283 ms vs ql-baseline-flamedart 10400/5100/5267. card-appearance: t3-fire-fire-water peakOPS 8.33 (23rd pct of 120 T3 cards; floor among offensive T3 burn). Corroborating code: static-confirmed.json entry #9 confirms Pyre-keyword detonators emit no stack_consumed, so they cannot advance Quench Lance's gate; cards.json effects confirm Quench Lance = [dot 2 burn(int); aura ttl6000 event_counter{stack_consumed,burn,min_amount:10} -> stat_gain int+2 max4] with NO direct damage, vs Flame Dart = [damage 5(dex); dot 2 burn].

finding.title: Burn-Supernova sub-line of detonator-control is fragile (loses lizard_king, thin on infernal_dragon); poison dual-DoT line is healthy
finding.evidence: archetype-summary boss3 detonator-control/mage: bossMeanWinRate 0.50. mage-detonator-control-boss3-1 (burn-bank-into-Supernova) vs lizard_king: winRate 0, 8/8 deaths, avgDamageDealt 403 of lizard_king's 407 scaled HP (dies ~4 HP short); its normals run slow at 22-24s TTK. The sibling poison/dual-DoT line mage-detonator-control-boss3-2 vs lizard_king WINS in 12.2s at 0.721 cushion and clears normals in 8-9s at 0.84-0.86 cushion. boss6 mage-detonator-control-boss6-2 (burn Supernova) vs infernal_dragon wins but at only 0.259 cushion (33s TTK).
finding.diagnosis: Within detonator-control the poison/Marsh-Squall dual-DoT line is fine; the weakness is specifically the burn-bank-into-Supernova line, which depends on a slow EXHAUST detonator and the same under-converting burn detonators (Venom Detonation/Cinderlance) flagged above. Against an enraging tempo boss (lizard_king enrage x1.5 at 30%, multi_hit) the burn pool matures too late — the 403-vs-407 near-miss shows it is a closing-speed problem, not a damage-floor problem. Fixing the burn detonators (above) directly remedies this sub-line; no control-card change needed.
finding.proposed_change: Resolve via the burn-detonator buffs already proposed (Venom Detonation 2->4 Pierce; Cinderlance cd 2.2->1.8 + 3->4 Pierce; Quench Lance bank buff). These give the burn-Supernova line the closing speed to convert before enrage. Do not nerf lizard_king (12 other archetypes clear it). No new defensive grafting onto control (C3).

verdict.title: CONFIRMED: burn-Supernova sub-line of detonator-control loses lizard_king on a closing-speed near-miss; finding's Venom Detonation 2->4 Pierce buff swap-tested and flips the loss to a win
verdict.sim_evidence: Data recheck (results-full.json / enemy-summary.json / archetype-summary.json), all quoted numbers accurate: mage-detonator-control-boss3-1 (burn-bank-into-Supernova) vs lizard_king = winRate 0, 8/8 deaths, avgDmg 403 vs lizard_king scaledHP 407 (dies ~4 HP short — a true near-miss). Sibling poison/dual-DoT line boss3-2 vs lizard_king wins 12.2s / cushion 0.721 / dmg 476. boss3-1 normals run 22.0-24.0s TTK (werewolf 22.7s, mutated_salamander 24.0s, baby_dragon 22.0s) vs boss3-2's 8-9s (werewolf 8.0s, salamander 9.0s, baby_dragon 8.0s) — burn-Supernova line ~2.5-3x slower at the SAME normals. lizard_king across all 22 matchups: meanWinRate 0.955, deathRate 0.045, affinity attack, enrage x1.5 @30%, multi_hit x2 — so this is a clear outlier loss (12+ archetypes clear it; do NOT nerf the boss). boss6: burn line boss6-2 vs infernal_dragon wins at cushion 0.259 / 33s TTK vs poison line boss6-1 at 0.599 / 13s — burn sub-line confirmed fragile. SWAP TEST (tests/audit/val/swap/det-burn-boss3.json + -res.json, boss3 params runXP1700/loopMult1.2/lizard_king/8 repeats, deck held in fixed order): baseline reproduced exactly (win 0, 8 deaths, dmg 403). venom4p (Venom Detonation t3-counter-fire-water effects[2] value 2->4 + pierce_armor, the finding's proposal): win 1.0, 0 deaths, dmg 407, TTK 47s, cushion 0.221 — FLIPS loss to win, no over-correction (slow 47s win, not trivial). superSnap (Supernova cd 2.8->1.8 + non-exhaust): also win 1.0 (dmg 409, cushion 0.151). super6 (Supernova per-burn value 5->6): STILL LOSES (win 0, 8 deaths, dmg 403) — proves it is a closing-SPEED problem, not a per-hit ceiling problem, exactly as the finding diagnoses.
verdict.reason: The finding is REAL and correctly characterized at the decisive level. (1) Every cited number is accurate against the analysis JSONs and I reproduced the baseline loss in-sim (win 0 / 8 deaths / dmg 403 vs lizard_king HP 407). (2) The core diagnosis is sound and sim-proven: it is a closing-speed near-miss against an enrage/multi_hit tempo boss, not a damage-floor failure — buffing Supernova's per-burn VALUE (5->6) did NOT fix it, while adding closing damage (Venom Detonation 2->4 Pierce) DID flip win 0->1.0 with only a +4 avgDmg swing. (3) The proposed change works and is C1-C5 compliant: it buffs a detonator's value + adds Pierce (no cost increase, no CD lengthening), grafts no defense/heal onto control (C3), and does not nerf lizard_king (12+ other archetypes clear it at high cushion). (4) The win is non-oppressive (47s TTK, 0.221 cushion). Two caveats keep this at MED, not high, and correct the finding's incidental mechanism rather than its conclusion: (a) The finding's claim that the weakness is 'the same under-converting burn detonators (Venom Detonation/Cinderlance)' is partly inaccurate — per static-confirmed bugs #5/#6, Venom Detonation is a Pyre-keyword detonator that reads the LIVE burn pool and self-fuels its own applied burn, so it does NOT under-convert; and Cinderlance (t3-attack-fire-fire) is NOT in the boss3-1 deck at all, so a Cinderlance CD buff cannot remedy THIS loss. The real structural bottleneck is a slow burn-bank engine (Spark/Flame Dart/Pyre/Cinder Sprint) feeding a one-shot EXHAUST Supernova (cd 2.8) whose pool matures after enrage. (b) Buffing Venom Detonation does help (swap-confirmed), so the proposed lever is valid even though the mechanistic story behind it is imperfect. Net: the archetype_weak finding stands, the proposed fix is validated by swap test, severity med is appropriate (single fragile sub-line, narrow boss, marginal near-miss; the sibling poison line and 6 other detonator-control boss matchups are healthy).

finding.title: poison+block and heal-sustain mages clear bosses at ~0.95-1.0 cushion — safe-but-slow, the intended C3 profile (NOT the 'can't close tanky bosses' problem)
finding.evidence: archetype-summary poison+block/mage: boss2 cushion 1.0, boss3 0.997, boss4 1.0, boss5 0.998 (all winRate 1). heal-sustain/mage: boss1 0.855, boss2 0.978, boss3 0.959 (all winRate 1). These contradict the brief's lead that 'defensive/armor MAGE hybrids can't close tanky bosses' — in the sim they DO close, comfortably, just slowly. The genuinely weak defensive-leaning mage is pure_burn (covered separately), not poison+block/heal-sustain.
finding.diagnosis: Pairing a defensive shell with a real second win condition (poison DoT, or heal+poison) produces the C3-intended outcome: high survival, full clears, no race pressure. They are safe-but-slow, not oppressive (single-target, no broad armor/heal->damage conversion). The brief's 'can't close' concern is NOT reproduced for these specific hybrids; it applies to pure_burn's defenseless race.
finding.proposed_change: NO CHANGE (C3-compliant). Methodology note for the synthesizer: do not treat the ~1.0 cushions here as a dominance flag — these archetypes win by surviving and out-DoT-ing, exactly as C3 intends, and they are not oppressive. The real defensive-mage weakness is pure_burn (see archetype_weak finding), which lacks any second win condition.

verdict.title: poison+block and heal-sustain mages clear all bosses at ~0.95-1.0 cushion via safe-but-slow survive-and-out-DoT — the intended C3 profile, NOT oppression and NOT the "can't close tanky bosses" problem (which is pure_burn)
verdict.reason: CONFIRMED. Every cited number checks out exactly against archetype-summary.json: poison+block/mage bossMeanCushion = boss2 1.0, boss3 0.997, boss4 1.0, boss5 0.998 (all bossMeanWinRate 1); heal-sustain/mage = boss1 0.855, boss2 0.978, boss3 0.959 (all bossMeanWinRate 1). All these boss matchups show deaths 0, timeouts 0 — so the archetypes DO close, directly contradicting the brief's lead that 'defensive/armor MAGE hybrids can't close tanky bosses.' The diagnosis (safe-but-slow, C3-intended, not oppressive, no change) follows the data and is corroborated by the per-matchup results and deck contents. The genuinely weak defensive-leaning mage is correctly identified as pure_burn, which the data confirms is fragile (pure_burn boss1 bossMeanWinRate 0.5, one matchup mage-pure-burn-boss1-2 with 8 deaths / cushion null; the surviving matchup wins on a razor 0.008 cushion). No small-sample or deck-quality confound undermines a NO-CHANGE verdict: 2 decks x 8 repeats per stage is modest but cushion ~1.0 holds consistently across 4 distinct deep bosses, and the decks are well-built. Proposed_change (NO CHANGE) is C1-C5 compliant.
verdict.sim_evidence: Re-read of results-full.json (the source sim, repeats=8) confirms the safe-but-slow C3 signature and proves the wins are survival-driven, not oppressive output: mage-poison-block-boss2-1 vs BOSS_iron_golem (enemyScaledHP 420) = winRate 1, deaths 0, timeouts 0, avgHeroHpPctOnWin 1.0 (zero damage taken), but avgTtkMs 20000 (the max clock) with avgDamageDealt only 435 over the full ~20s. mage-heal-sustain-boss2-1 vs BOSS_iron_golem = winRate 1, deaths 0, cushion 1.0, avgTtkMs 20000, avgDamageDealt 438. Identical pattern: full survival, maximum grind time, modest single-target output — the textbook 'race-losing but elite at survival, buy time for a second win condition (poison DoT)' C3 outcome. Contrast same boss (420 HP): pure_burn clears at cushion 0.281, pure_poison at 0.871 — i.e. the defensive hybrids trade ALL of their speed for total safety rather than dominating with power. Deck inspection (decks-full.json) confirms compliance: mage-poison-block-boss2-1 'Brineward Outlast' is water(poison)+defense(block) T3s; mage-heal-sustain-boss1-1 mixes water poison + heal cards. The heal source t3-water-water-water 'Tidesong Aura' is heal+stat_gain+mana with NO heal->damage conversion, so there is no forbidden broad armor/heal->damage line. The pure_burn contrast is verified in archetype-summary.json: pure_burn/mage boss1 bossMeanWinRate 0.5, bossMeanCushion 0.008, with matchup mage-pure-burn-boss1-2 vs doom_knight at winRate 0 / deaths 8.

finding.title: Riposte is a negative-value attack slot — slower and lower-cushion than the basic T1 Jab it competes with
finding.evidence: card-appearance: t1-counter peakOPS 0 vs the rest of the T1 stamina-ATTACK pack (Jab 24.17, Quickstep 26.67; pack median 24.17). Swap test (5 reps/enemy, early stage, fixed-order deck): replacing one Jab with Riposte made the deck STRICTLY worse on all 4 early enemies — lost_lizard avgTTK 10500ms vs 8900ms, giant_spider 32100 vs 26900, corpse_eater 24900ms/cushion 0.282 vs 19300ms/cushion 0.521 (Riposte loses HALF the HP cushion), pocket_cat 30900ms/0.692 vs 22900ms/0.795. Card is 'For 6 seconds, every time you hit, deal 3' at 1 stamina / cd 2.0.
finding.diagnosis: Riposte is a 6s on-hit '+3 flat' aura (no STR scaling, no DEX scaling) on a 2.0s cooldown. The flat 3 is dwarfed by Jab's scaling hit, and the slow 2.0s cd means casting it costs a turn that a real Jab would have spent dealing damage — so its marginal value is NEGATIVE versus the deck's own filler. peakOPS reads 0 because in isolation it never lands a hit to ride. As the lone T1 'counter' attack, a player who picks it is strictly punished; it is unused-tier per the brief's priority concern.
finding.proposed_change: C1 rescue: cut cooldown to the 1.2s floor (matching Jab/Quickstep) AND give the on-hit bonus a DEX scaler so it tracks the hero (e.g. 'deal 3([dex])'). The card stays a clean T1 single-effect rider but at least breaks even as a deck slot. Do NOT raise its cost. If still under-pack after the CD/scaler fix, lengthen the aura window from 6s to 8s so more hits ride it. Re-run the same Jab-swap to confirm TTK/cushion reach parity, not exceed it (T1 ceiling).

verdict.title: Riposte is the weakest T1 stamina-attack — a real CD-driven efficiency gap, but NOT a negative-value/unused card; severity overstated
verdict.sim_evidence: Swap test, fixed-order early warrior deck (runXP 120 / loop2 / mult 1.0), Jab(t1-attack) vs Riposte(t1-counter) in slot 1, 6 reps/enemy. Spec tests/audit/val/swap/riposte-swap.json, results tests/audit/val/swap/riposte-swap-res.json. Per-enemy (Jab / Riposte): lost_lizard TTK 4900/5700, cushion 0.974/0.974; giant_spider TTK 12100/13700, cushion 0.855/0.829; corpse_eater TTK 9700/12500, cushion 0.650/0.479; pocket_cat TTK 12100/13700, cushion 0.726/0.675. Aggregate (4 enemies): Jab meanWR 1.000 / TTK 9700 / cushion 0.801; Riposte meanWR 1.000 / TTK 11400 (+18%) / cushion 0.739 (-0.062). winRate stays 1.0 with ZERO deaths/timeouts everywhere — Riposte never loses a fight, it just wins slower with slightly less cushion. Buffed variant (cd 2.0->1.2 + DEX scaler '3([dex])'): meanWR 1.000 / TTK 10000 / cushion 0.780 — matches Jab on 3/4 enemies (lost_lizard/giant_spider/pocket_cat TTK identical to Jab) and improves corpse_eater (TTK 10900<12500, cushion 0.564>0.479) WITHOUT exceeding Jab (10000>=9700, 0.780<=0.801), so it reaches parity, not over-correction (respects T1 ceiling). Note: at runXP 120 the warrior has DEX 0 (XPSystem getLevel(120)=L2, HeroStatsResolver floor(L/4)=0), so the DEX scaler contributed nothing here — the entire parity fix came from the CD cut alone; warrior DEX only reaches 1 at L5-7 and 2 at L8, so the scaler is a minor late add. Real multi-deck appearance data (card-appearance.json) over 8 decks/44 matchups: Riposte meanNormalWinRate 0.977, meanNormalCushion 0.877, meanBossWinRate 1.0 — statistically indistinguishable from Jab (0.993/0.898, 63 decks) and Quickstep (0.987/0.870, 30 decks). peakOPS 0 is an isolation artifact (an on_hit_dealt aura measured alone never lands a hit to ride), which the finding itself acknowledges.
verdict.reason: The finding's DIRECTION is real and I reproduced it: Riposte is strictly dominated by Jab as a T1 stamina-attack deck slot — slower TTK and slightly lower cushion on every one of 4 early enemies, never better. Root cause is correct: the 2.0s cooldown (vs Jab/Quickstep 1.2s) and a flat unscaled 3-per-hit aura make casting it cost a turn that a real attack would have spent. The proposed C1 rescue (cut CD to the 1.2 floor, no cost increase, no T2/T3 nerf) WORKS and is C1-compliant — my buffed variant reaches Jab parity without over-shooting the T1 ceiling. So the card is genuinely the off-curve weakling of its pack and the fix is sound. HOWEVER the finding is mis-characterized on severity and specifics, so I downgrade high->low: (1) 'negative-value slot' / 'unused-tier' / 'high severity' is wrong — winRate is 1.0 with zero deaths/timeouts in every matchup, and across 8 real library decks Riposte's winRate 0.977 / cushion 0.877 / bossWR 1.0 are indistinguishable from Jab and Quickstep. It costs speed/cushion, not wins; it is a minor efficiency gap, not a dead/unused card. (2) 'loses HALF the HP cushion' is an exaggeration sourced from a weaker deck — the finding's quoted corpse_eater 0.521/0.282 and TTKs 2-3x larger than mine indicate a low-power baseline (deck-quality confound); in a fair fixed-runXP swap the worst case is 0.650->0.479 (~26% relative) and the aggregate drop is only ~8% (0.801->0.739). (3) the DEX-scaler half of the proposed buff is near-useless at the stage tested (DEX 0 at loop2) — the CD cut is the load-bearing fix; the scaler is harmless but over-credited. Net: a legitimate low-severity off-curve-weak / parity issue with a correct, C1-compliant CD-cut fix; confirmed but not high.

finding.title: Razor Stance is the weakest T2 attack — slower than the Reckless Strike it replaces and standalone-dead (peakOPS 0)
finding.evidence: card-appearance: t2-counter-counter peakOPS 0, the bottom of the T2 stamina-ATTACK pack (median 25.42, max 55.83). Swap test (5 reps/enemy, mid stage): swapping Reckless Strike -> Razor Stance was SLOWER on all 5 mid enemies — mutated_salamander 12900ms vs 8900ms, venomous_kobra 12660 vs 7880, lava_golen 12900 vs 8900, skeleton 11700 vs 7700, werewolf 11700 vs 7700 (≈+4000-5000ms each) — and lower cushion on 4 of 5. Card: 'For 10s, every hit applies 1[bleed]([dex]). Vengeance: +4s' at 1 stamina / cd 2.0.
finding.diagnosis: Razor Stance is a bleed-on-hit aura with a 2.0s cooldown and no direct damage; peakOPS 0 reflects that it does nothing on cast without other attacks landing. Its bleed-per-hit needs many cheap hits to pay off, but its own 2.0s cd burns a turn that Reckless Strike (cd 1.2, +9 dmg, +3 rage, self-bleed) would have spent attacking, so in fixed-order play it loses ~4-5s of TTK. It is a legitimate enabler but is mis-priced on the snappiness lever, not the cost lever. NOTE: per static-confirmed, its 'Vengeance: +4 seconds' clause is actually a SECOND concurrent bleed aura (double-bleed for 4s), not a duration extension — that desc bug is out-of-scope here but means its real in-deck value is slightly higher than the text implies.
finding.proposed_change: C1 rescue: cut cooldown 2.0 -> 1.4 so arming the aura no longer sacrifices a full attack turn (this is the primary cause of the ~4s TTK loss). Keep cost at 1 stamina and keep the bleed-per-hit body. This brings it toward the T2 attack pack without touching Reckless Strike. Re-run the Reckless-swap at mid; target TTK within ~1s of Reckless, not faster (T2 ceiling).

verdict.title: Razor Stance is the weakest T2 attack — slower than the Reckless Strike it replaces and standalone-dead (peakOPS 0)
verdict.reason: CONFIRMED, med. Data recheck: card-appearance confirms t2-counter-counter peakOPS 0, the bottom of the T2 stamina-ATTACK pack. I recomputed the pack (n=12) and reproduced the finding's exact figures — median peakOPS 25.42, max 55.83 (Reckless Strike); Razor Stance is dead last at 0 (next-lowest Crimson Tithe 3.75). Card def matches the finding: cost 1 stamina, cd 2.0, two on_hit_dealt bleed auras and ZERO direct damage. The static-confirmed "Vengeance +4s = second concurrent bleed aura, gated on took_damage_within_ms:2000" is real and correctly flagged as out-of-scope (and it does NOT arm on an opening cast, since the hero hasn't been hit yet — part of why standalone OPS is 0). Swap-test sim (my own, 6 reps/enemy, realistic attack-heavy mid bleed deck, candidate in slot 0, 5 mid enemies): swapping Reckless Strike -> Razor Stance is SLOWER on all 5 with lower cushion on all 5 — mutated_salamander 9100->13500ms (cush .793->.657), venomous_kobra 8217->12033, lava_golen 9100->12000, skeleton 6500->10000, werewolf 7700->11900 (mean +3763ms). Direction and conclusion match the finding (it cited +4-5s; my realistic deck shows +2.9-4.4s — same conclusion, slightly smaller magnitude). Extra rigor (additive test vs a FREE T1 jab in slot 0 with the attack body unchanged): Razor cd2.0 is tied-or-slower than a basic T1 jab on all 3 enemies (salamander 11500 vs 13100; kobra 9317 vs 10733; werewolf 9100 vs 9000) — a T2 that cannot out-TTK a T1 filler is genuinely below-curve. Severity stays MED not high because win rate is 1.0 with no deaths/timeouts everywhere (in-deck card-appearance: win 1.0, cushion 0.876, boss 1.0 over 12 decks); this is an unused-tier/snappiness problem, not a loss-causing one. CAVEAT on the proposal: it is C1-compliant (cut CD on the snappiness lever, keep 1 stamina, do NOT touch Reckless) and directionally validated — cd 2.0->1.4 recovers a mean 1760ms (47% of the gap closed) and lifts cushion on 4/5 — but its stated TARGET ("TTK within ~1s of Reckless") is NOT met by the CD cut alone: Razor stays ~+2003ms (mean) slower than Reckless and only reaches rough parity with a T1 jab. cd1.4 does not overcorrect (never faster than Reckless, win still 1.0, not oppressive). Recommend accepting the cd cut as the C1 rescue but noting it lands Razor at the bottom-mid of the T2 pack, not at its ceiling; the finding's "within ~1s" claim is optimistic. Sim artifacts: tests/audit/val/swap/razor-mid.json (+ -res.json) and tests/audit/val/swap/razor-additive.json (+ -res.json).
verdict.sim_evidence: Swap test, 6 reps/enemy, mid (runXP 350, mult 1.0), attack-heavy bleed warrior, candidate slot 0. BASELINE Reckless -> CURRENT Razor cd2.0 -> FIX Razor cd1.4 (avgTtkMs / cushion): mutated_salamander 9100/.793 -> 13500/.657 -> 12300/.714; venomous_kobra 8217/.840 -> 12033/.801 -> 10633/.793; lava_golen 9100/.814 -> 12000/.743 -> 10700/.757; skeleton 6500/.879 -> 10000/.843 -> 8000/.843; werewolf 7700/.814 -> 11900/.757 -> 9000/.821. Win 1.00, 0 deaths, 0 timeouts in ALL 15 matchups. Mean TTK gap vs Reckless: current +3763ms, fix14 +2003ms; cd1.4 recovers 1760ms (47% of gap). ADDITIVE test (Razor vs a free T1 jab in slot 0, body unchanged): salamander jab 11500 vs Razor cd2.0 13100 / cd1.4 11900; kobra 9317 vs 10733 / 10167; werewolf 9100 vs 9000 / 8000 — Razor is tied-or-slower than a T1 filler. Pack recheck: T2 stamina-attack peakOPS median 25.42, max 55.83 (Reckless), Razor Stance 0 (last).

finding.title: Cleaver's Tax reads as 'stronger than its text' (dead CD downside) but is NOT off-curve-strong by output — do not nerf it
finding.evidence: static-confirmed: overload_lockout_ms:4000 is unwired, so the '...delays 4 more seconds next time' drawback never fires (sim cooldownDebtSec=0 even at rage>=5 firing 42 dmg). Yet card-appearance peakOPS for t3-attack-counter-counter is only 28.0 — BELOW the T3 stamina-attack median (33.64) and far under top T3 attacks (Vein Splitter 134.38, Razor Cadence 72.75). In-deck cushion 0.896, WR 1.0, nDecks 12 — unremarkable.
finding.diagnosis: The missing downside makes Cleaver's Tax mechanically stronger than its printed text, but in full-combat output it is still only a mid-pack T3 attack, not oppressive. The right fix is the WIRING fix flagged in static-confirmed (route overload_lockout_ms into cooldownDebtSec OR drop the clause from the text) — a correctness fix, not a balance nerf. I am explicitly NOT recommending a cost/CD nerf: raising its cost would push a merely-average T3 below pack and create a new off-curve-weak card. Logging this so the synthesizer does not double-count the static bug as a balance over-strong finding.
finding.proposed_change: No balance change. Defer to static-confirmed's correctness fix (wire overload_lockout_ms via cd_debt or remove the clause). If the downside is wired, re-measure: if peakOPS drops materially below the T3 pack afterward, compensate by cutting its CD 2.0 -> 1.8 (C1) rather than touching cost.

verdict.title: Cleaver's Tax reads 'stronger than its text' (dead overload_lockout downside) but is NOT off-curve-strong by full-combat output — defer to the static wiring fix, no balance nerf
verdict.sim_evidence:
SWAP TEST (tests/audit/val/swap/cleavers-tax-verify.json -> -mu.json -> -res.json, boss2 band runXP 1150, warrior rage/attack deck, candidate in slot 3 after two rage-builders so the rage>=5 gate is met; 6 repeats; vs werewolf, ancient_tree, fire_elemental, iron_golem boss).

(A) Marginal value vs a T2 filler (Reckless Strike) baseline — confirms a REAL upgrade but NOT oppressive: ancient_tree (high def) avgDamageDealt 123 (filler) -> 197 (Cleaver's Tax), a +74 single-cast Pierce burst (the rage>=5 bonus + STR scale firing); fire_elemental 127 -> 199; werewolf ttk 4100ms/4 cards -> 2900ms/3 cards. But baseline ALREADY wins every matchup at WR 1.0, so the card trivializes nothing new; on the iron_golem boss it barely moves the needle: WR 1.0 both, cushion 0.70 (filler) -> 0.73 (Cleaver's Tax), ttk 11700 -> 11300ms.

(B) Current vs downside-WIRED (replaced effects[] to add a cd_debt +4s gated on the same self_stack_atleast rage:5): IDENTICAL across all four enemies — WR 1.00/1.00, ttk 2900/2900 (normals) & 11300/11300 (boss), cushion 0.94/0.94 & 0.73/0.73, dmg 197/197 (normals) & 443/443 (boss). Even in the one fight long enough for a 'next time +4s' lockout to bite (iron_golem, 9-card/~11s), wiring the dead downside changes nothing measurable. This empirically confirms the correct static fix needs NO balance compensation.
verdict.reason: The finding is real and correctly characterized on every load-bearing claim. (1) STATIC BUG CONFIRMED: overload_lockout_ms:4000 (cards.json:2402) is read nowhere in the engine — cooldownDebtSec is produced ONLY by the cd_debt effect type (CardResolver.ts:786-790), and Cleaver's Tax has no cd_debt effect, so the '...delays 4 more seconds next time' clause is purely cosmetic and the card is mechanically stronger than its text. Three independent static-confirmed entries agree. (2) ALL OPS NUMBERS VERIFIED, none invented: peakOPS 28.0, cushion 0.896, WR 1.0, meanBossWinRate 1.0, nDecks 12 (card-appearance.json:1102-1118); the cited 'T3 stamina-attack median 33.64' is exactly reproducible (I computed it from card-appearance: 33 T3 stamina attacks, median 33.64, mean 37.54, and Cleaver's Tax ranks 12/33 — below both median and mean, with 11 T3 attacks scoring lower); Vein Splitter peakOPS 134.38 and Razor Cadence 72.75 confirmed exactly. So Cleaver's Tax is genuinely a mid-pack-to-below-median T3 attack, not off-curve-strong. (3) SWAP TEST CONFIRMS NOT OPPRESSIVE and confirms the dead downside is immaterial (current == downside-wired byte-for-byte). The finding's recommendation — no balance change, defer to the static wiring fix, and explicitly do NOT raise cost (C1: that would push a below-median card further below pack and create a new off-curve-weak card) — is correct and C1-C5-compliant. The only nuance: the finding's contingency 'if peakOPS drops materially after wiring, cut CD 2.0->1.8' is supported by my sim, which shows wiring the downside does NOT materially change output, so even that compensatory CD cut is unlikely to be needed. Severity low is right: this is a correctness/clarity issue (already captured by static-confirmed), not a balance lever, and the caveat's real purpose — preventing the synthesizer from double-counting the static bug as an over-strong balance finding — is valid.

finding.target: physical defense cards (T1-T3, category=defense, stamina) — low peakOPS is by design, not off-curve
finding.title: Defense-category cards' low peakOPS must NOT be read as off-curve-weak (C3: armor/heal are intentionally race-losing)
finding.evidence: The physical defense pack reads far below attack OPS (e.g. t1-defense Guard 6, t2-agility-defense 6.15, t3-defense-defense-defense Aegis 14, many T3 defenses 5-10), while in-deck cushion is uniformly high (0.93-1.0, e.g. Guard cushion 0.935, Aegis 1.0, Thornwall 0.999, Bramble Bulwark 1.0). tier-ops-perf medians (T2 15.57 / T3 15.33) are pulled down precisely by these defense cards.
finding.diagnosis: peakOPS is isolated DAMAGE-per-second, so a card that outputs armor/heal/stun scores low by construction — exactly what C3 mandates (defense is elite at survival, race-losing on damage). Comparing a defense card's OPS against the attack pack would mis-flag the entire defensive suite as 'weak'. The honest read is: defense cards must be judged on cushion/survival contribution (where they are saturated at ~1.0) and on defense-vs-defense OPS, not against attacks. No defense card in scope shows an off-curve cushion deficit. This caveat also explains why the headline winRate/cushion fields in card-appearance barely discriminate physical cards (fixed-order decks nearly all win) — TTK swap-tests, not those saturated fields, are the discriminating signal used above.
finding.proposed_change: No card change. Methodological note for the synthesizer: do not treat low-OPS defense cards as off-curve-weak; off-curve detection for physical cards should rest on (a) attack-vs-attack peakOPS pack position and (b) marginal TTK/cushion in swap tests, both of which were used for the weak findings above.

verdict.target: physical defense cards (T1-T3, category=defense, stamina) — low peakOPS is by design, not off-curve
verdict.title: Defense-category cards' low peakOPS must NOT be read as off-curve-weak (C3: armor/heal/stun are intentionally race-losing)
verdict.reason: CONFIRMED as a valid methodology caveat. Verified directly against card-appearance.json and tier-ops-perf.json; no swap-test needed because the finding proposes NO card change — it is about how to interpret OPS for an entire category, which I checked against the data. (1) peakOPS is isolated DAMAGE-per-second, so armor/heal/stun cards score low by construction — exactly what WORKFLOW-BRIEF C3 mandates ('armor/heal/stun are INTENTIONALLY race-losing'). (2) The attack-vs-defense OPS gap is large and consistent at every tier (recomputed medians: T1 attack 24.17 vs defense 6; T2 attack 25.42 vs defense 7; T3 attack 28.92 vs defense 8.67), so comparing defense OPS against attack OPS would mis-flag the whole defensive suite as weak. (3) Defense cards are saturated on their actual charter (survival): meanNormalCushion 0.878-1.0 for the 19 defense cards with nDecks>=5, NONE below 0.85, headline normal winRate ~1.0 and bossWin 1.0 across the board — no off-curve cushion deficit exists. (4) Named cards verified via cards-index: t1-defense=Guard (OPS 6, cush 0.935), t3-defense-defense-defense=Aegis of Returning Wrath (OPS 14, cush 1.0), t2-counter-earth=Thornwall (OPS 11.7, cush 0.999), t2-defense-earth=Bramble Bulwark (OPS 10, cush 1.0) — all as cited. (5) Static-confirmed bugs do not duplicate this caveat. The proposed_change (no card edit; synthesizer note to judge defense on cushion/defense-vs-defense OPS, not against attacks) is fully C1-C5 compliant. Severity correctly LOW (it is a guardrail against false positives, not a balance defect). Minor accuracy note: the finding quotes prior-audit medians T2 15.57 / T3 15.33; my fresh recompute from card-appearance gives T2 16 / T3 16.5 — slightly different source/rounding but directionally identical and the conclusion is unchanged.
verdict.sim_evidence: No swap-test run (finding proposes no card change). Direct data recheck: card-appearance OPS by tier x category — T1 attack median 24.17 (n=3) vs defense 6 (n=1); T2 attack 25.42 (n=12) vs defense 7 (n=9); T3 attack 28.92 (n=51) vs defense 8.67 (n=46). Critically, removing defense cards restores T3>T2: T3 median jumps from 16.5 (all, n=120) to 25.42 (non-defense, n=74), while T2 goes 16 (all, n=36) to 18.75 (non-defense, n=27) — proving the pooled-median 'T3 not higher than T2' anomaly is an artifact of T3 carrying 46 race-losing defense cards vs T2's 9. Defense cushion saturation: 19 defense cards with nDecks>=5 span cushion 0.878-1.0, zero below 0.85; e.g. Guard 0.935, Aegis 1.0, Thornwall 0.999, Bramble Bulwark 1.0, Stoneward Reprisal 1.0 — all with normal winRate 1.0 and bossWin 1.0.
